questions.prompt: The report's [[1]] conclusions — careful to acknowledge every qualification — stand in stark contrast to the [[2]] assertions of the summary document, which appears designed for an audience [[3]] of engaging with the underlying complexity.
questions.explanation: Report is careful and qualified = tentative. Summary is the opposite = categorical. Audience for the simplified summary must be incapable of engaging with complexity — hence the simplification.

questions.prompt: The architect's reputation for [[1]] was not entirely deserved; many of her allegedly improvised solutions were in fact [[2]] in private notebooks years before the client's brief ever arrived.
questions.explanation: Reputation for spontaneity = improvising on the spot. But solutions were actually sketched years earlier — premeditated, not improvised.

questions.prompt: The minister's resignation statement, widely praised for its [[1]], was in fact a masterpiece of strategic vagueness: it admitted nothing, promised nothing, and committed its author to no particular course of action.
questions.explanation: Praised for candor (openness/honesty) — but ironically it was actually evasive. The gap between reputation and reality is the passage's point.

questions.prompt: That the author chose to [[1]] the most damaging testimony in a footnote rather than address it in the main text suggests a [[2]] that undermines the very [[3]] of objectivity his preface so conspicuously claims.
questions.explanation: Hiding damaging testimony in a footnote = burying it. This reveals partiality (bias). It undermines the pose (pretense) of objectivity — the preface only claims objectivity, it doesn't enact it.

questions.prompt: The committee's decision to release the report without [[1]] was [[2]]; in the absence of contextual framing, readers drew precisely the alarming conclusions that the organization had spent years trying to prevent.
questions.explanation: No commentary = no framing. The result — alarming conclusions — was the opposite of the organization's intent = counterproductive.

questions.prompt: The essayist's prose is celebrated for its [[1]] — the ability to compress a complex argument into a phrase so precisely calibrated that expansion would only dilute it.
questions.explanation: Compressing complex arguments into precisely calibrated phrases = extreme brevity and efficiency. Concision and economy both name this quality.

questions.prompt: The union's [[1]] position — refusing to negotiate any clause touching on automation regardless of the economic context — made agreement impossible even when management offered substantial concessions.
questions.explanation: Refusing to negotiate regardless of context or concessions = completely inflexible. Intransigent and obdurate both mean stubbornly refusing to yield.

questions.prompt: Her assessment was [[1]], arriving at exactly the conclusion the evidence supported and no further, without the embellishments of certainty that more ambitious analysts typically add.
questions.explanation: Arriving at exactly what evidence supports, no further = restrained and careful. Measured and circumspect both describe this calibrated, non-overreaching stance.

questions.prompt: The introduction of competitive markets into sectors long dominated by state monopoly produced results that were, at best, [[1]]: some inefficiencies were reduced while others merely changed form.
questions.explanation: Results that reduced some inefficiencies while changing the form of others = mixed, inconclusive. Ambivalent and equivocal both capture this hard-to-evaluate quality.

questions.prompt: In writing the emperor's biography from the perspective of household servants, the historian produced an account that is [[1]] without being disrespectful — revealing without descending into gossip.
questions.explanation: 'Without being disrespectful' and 'revealing without gossip' defines a careful middle ground. Intimate and candid both describe the closeness and honesty that stop short of sensationalism.

questions.passage:
The field of behavioral genetics has established, with reasonable consistency, that most psychological traits have substantial heritability — meaning that a significant proportion of variation in a given trait among individuals in a population can be attributed to genetic differences. This finding is widely misunderstood in two directions: by those who take it to imply that genes determine traits absolutely, and by those who take it to imply nothing at all because environments are known to matter.

Heritability is a population statistic, not a claim about individual development. The same trait can have high heritability in one population and low heritability in another, depending on how much environmental variation is present. If all individuals share a highly uniform environment, the remaining variation will be almost entirely genetic in origin, yielding high heritability — even if an individual raised in a very different environment would develop the trait differently. Policy implications that follow from heritability estimates are therefore highly sensitive to assumptions about the environmental context in which those estimates were obtained.
questions.prompt: The passage suggests that high heritability for a trait in a population
options: implies that the trait cannot be altered by environmental intervention
means that genes are more important than environments in determining the trait
may reflect the uniformity of the environment in which the estimate was obtained
is a necessary condition for the trait to be classified as psychological
implies that the same estimate would be obtained in any population studied
questions.answer: may reflect the uniformity of the environment in which the estimate was obtained
questions.explanation: The passage states directly: if environment is uniform, remaining variation is genetic, yielding high heritability — 'even if' a different environment would produce different development.

questions.passage:
The field of behavioral genetics has established, with reasonable consistency, that most psychological traits have substantial heritability — meaning that a significant proportion of variation in a given trait among individuals in a population can be attributed to genetic differences. This finding is widely misunderstood in two directions: by those who take it to imply that genes determine traits absolutely, and by those who take it to imply nothing at all because environments are known to matter.

Heritability is a population statistic, not a claim about individual development. The same trait can have high heritability in one population and low heritability in another, depending on how much environmental variation is present. If all individuals share a highly uniform environment, the remaining variation will be almost entirely genetic in origin, yielding high heritability — even if an individual raised in a very different environment would develop the trait differently. Policy implications that follow from heritability estimates are therefore highly sensitive to assumptions about the environmental context in which those estimates were obtained.
questions.prompt: The primary purpose of the passage is to
options: argue that heritability estimates should not be used to inform policy decisions
defend behavioral genetics against critics who deny any role for genes
clarify a commonly misunderstood statistical concept and identify its implications
demonstrate that environmental variation is the primary driver of psychological traits
survey methodological debates within behavioral genetics research
questions.answer: clarify a commonly misunderstood statistical concept and identify its implications
questions.explanation: First paragraph identifies two misunderstandings; second paragraph explains what heritability actually means and closes with policy implications.

questions.passage:
The field of behavioral genetics has established, with reasonable consistency, that most psychological traits have substantial heritability — meaning that a significant proportion of variation in a given trait among individuals in a population can be attributed to genetic differences. This finding is widely misunderstood in two directions: by those who take it to imply that genes determine traits absolutely, and by those who take it to imply nothing at all because environments are known to matter.

Heritability is a population statistic, not a claim about individual development. The same trait can have high heritability in one population and low heritability in another, depending on how much environmental variation is present. If all individuals share a highly uniform environment, the remaining variation will be almost entirely genetic in origin, yielding high heritability — even if an individual raised in a very different environment would develop the trait differently. Policy implications that follow from heritability estimates are therefore highly sensitive to assumptions about the environmental context in which those estimates were obtained.
questions.prompt: The author describes misunderstandings as occurring 'in two directions' primarily to suggest that
options: genetic and environmental determinism are equally dangerous misconceptions
only trained scientists can correctly interpret heritability findings
both over- and underinterpretation of heritability findings produce errors
the correct view lies precisely halfway between the two extremes
heritability findings are inherently prone to political misuse
questions.answer: both over- and underinterpretation of heritability findings produce errors
questions.explanation: The two directions are: overclaiming (genes determine everything) and underclaiming (heritability implies nothing). Both are wrong — the author's point is that misunderstanding runs both ways.

questions.passage:
The emergence of print capitalism transformed not merely the speed at which information traveled but the social conditions under which communities could imagine themselves as communities. Benedict Anderson's argument that nations are 'imagined communities' — bound together by shared consumption of print media rather than face-to-face interaction — depends on the observation that newspapers and novels created a sense of simultaneity: readers in distant cities could feel that they inhabited a common moment, experiencing the same events.

Critics have noted that the theory struggles to account for pre-print forms of collective identity — tribal, religious, and dynastic loyalties that generated intense group feeling without mass literacy. Some propose that Anderson's theory should be understood as describing not the origin of collective identity as such, but the specific form it takes under conditions of mass literacy — a more modest claim that preserves the theory's insight while limiting its scope.
questions.prompt: The critics mentioned in the second paragraph would most likely agree that Anderson's theory
options: accurately describes the origins of all significant forms of collective identity
should be abandoned in favor of accounts centered on face-to-face interaction
is more useful as a description of modern national identity than of identity in general
fails to explain why print capitalism produced different national forms in different regions
overestimates the role of simultaneity in creating solidarity among dispersed readers
questions.answer: is more useful as a description of modern national identity than of identity in general
questions.explanation: The critics' proposed revision — Anderson describes identity 'under conditions of mass literacy' rather than identity 'as such' — restricts his theory to print-era national identity, not all collective identity.

questions.passage:
The emergence of print capitalism transformed not merely the speed at which information traveled but the social conditions under which communities could imagine themselves as communities. Benedict Anderson's argument that nations are 'imagined communities' — bound together by shared consumption of print media rather than face-to-face interaction — depends on the observation that newspapers and novels created a sense of simultaneity: readers in distant cities could feel that they inhabited a common moment, experiencing the same events.

Critics have noted that the theory struggles to account for pre-print forms of collective identity — tribal, religious, and dynastic loyalties that generated intense group feeling without mass literacy. Some propose that Anderson's theory should be understood as describing not the origin of collective identity as such, but the specific form it takes under conditions of mass literacy — a more modest claim that preserves the theory's insight while limiting its scope.
options: the speed at which print technology distributed information across large territories
the shared sense among geographically dispersed readers of inhabiting a common present
the simultaneous development of print industries in different European nations
the feeling that newspapers accurately reflected current events as they unfolded
the synchronization of calendar and clock time made possible by printed almanacs
questions.answer: the shared sense among geographically dispersed readers of inhabiting a common present
questions.explanation: 'Readers in distant cities could feel that they inhabited a common moment, experiencing the same events' — that is the simultaneity Anderson identifies as the foundation of national community.
